text: The sugar demand equation is \(Q_D = 50 - 10P\). Which statement best describes the relationship shown by this equation?

options.text: As price rises, quantity demanded rises.
As price rises, quantity demanded falls.
Price does not affect quantity demanded.
The demand curve slopes upward because buyers respond to incentives.

feedback: The coefficient on \(P\) is negative: each €1 increase in price reduces quantity demanded by 10 million kilograms. That is the law of demand.

text: Using \(Q_D = 50 - 10P\), what is quantity demanded when \(P = 3.5\)? Enter the answer in millions of kilograms.
feedback: Substitute \(P = 3.5\): \(Q_D = 50 - 10(3.5) = 50 - 35 = 15\). Quantity demanded is 15 million kilograms.

text: Which of the following corresponds to the inverse demand equation when demand is \(Q_D = 50 - 10P\)?
The sugar supply equation is \(Q_S = 15P\). What happens to quantity supplied when price rises?

options.text: Quantity supplied rises because higher prices make production more attractive.
Quantity supplied falls because higher prices reduce producer revenue.
Quantity supplied stays fixed because supply is independent of price.
Quantity supplied becomes negative at higher prices.

feedback: The coefficient on \(P\) is positive. In this model, each €1 increase in price increases quantity supplied by 15 million kilograms.

text: Using \(Q_S = 15P\), what is quantity supplied when \(P = 3\)? Enter the answer in millions of kilograms.
feedback: Substitute \(P = 3\): \(Q_S = 15(3) = 45\). Quantity supplied is 45 million kilograms.

text: Which of the following corresponds to the inverse supply equation when supply is \(Q_S = 15P\)?
feedback: Divide both sides of \(Q_S = 15P\) by 15 to isolate price: \(P = (1/15)Q_S\).

text: Find the equilibrium price when \(Q_D = 72 - 8P\) and \(Q_S = 12P\).
feedback: Set quantity demanded equal to quantity supplied: \(72 - 8P = 12P\). Then \(72 = 20P\), so \(P^* = 3.6\).

text: Find the equilibrium quantity when \(Q_D = 60 - 6P\) and \(Q_S = 4P\). Enter the answer in millions of kilograms.
feedback: Set \(60 - 6P = 4P\), so \(60 = 10P\) and \(P^* = 6\). Substitute back in: \(Q_S = 4(6) = 24\), so equilibrium quantity is 24 million kilograms.

text: At \(P = 5\), \(Q_D = 30\) and \(Q_S = 50\). What market condition results, and what pressure does it create?

options.text: A shortage of 20 million kilograms; upward pressure on price.
A surplus of 20 million kilograms; downward pressure on price.
A surplus of 80 million kilograms; upward pressure on price.
No imbalance; the market is in equilibrium.

feedback: Quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded by \(50 - 30 = 20\). Unsold output creates a surplus, which normally puts downward pressure on price.

text: At \(P = 1\), \(Q_D = 40\) and \(Q_S = 15\). What market condition results, and what pressure does it create?

options.text: A surplus of 25 million kilograms; downward pressure on price.
A shortage of 25 million kilograms; upward pressure on price.
A shortage of 55 million kilograms; downward pressure on price.
No imbalance; quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.

feedback: Quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied by \(40 - 15 = 25\). Buyers want more than sellers provide, creating upward pressure on price.

text: Under a price floor at \(P = 4.5\), if \(Q_S = 54\) and \(Q_D = 27\), how many million kilograms of surplus are created?
feedback: The surplus is \(Q_S - Q_D = 54 - 27 = 27\). A price floor above equilibrium creates 27 million kilograms of excess supply.
